skill.name: western-blot-quantifier
skill.description: Automatically identify Western Blot gel bands, perform densitometric analysis, and calculate normalized values relative to loading controls.
skill.summary: Automated Western Blot gel band detection and densitometric normalization for biomedical research data analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 56 static analysis findings are false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts at scripts/main.py:54 and SKILL.md lines 3, 25, 131, 133, 221 are caused by argparse instantiation and markdown text (no actual crypto usage). The 40 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts on SKILL.md are standard markdown code fences for documentation examples, not shell command execution. Path traversal alerts at lines 246 and 249 cite security checklist items that discuss preventing traversal attacks. The hardcoded URL on line 7 is a legitimate GitHub source attribution. The init.py imports classes (WesternBlotQuantifier, AnalysisResult, BandRegion) that do not exist in main.py (which only defines WBQuantifier) - this is a code quality bug but not a security concern. No prompt injection, credential exposure, or malicious patterns detected.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak cryptographic algorithm on argparse
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged argparse.ArgumentParser() at scripts/main.py:54 as a 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. This is an argparse instantiation for CLI argument parsing, not cryptographic code. Line contains only standard ArgumentParser setup.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code at line 54 is unambiguously argparse boilerplate with zero cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak cryptographic algorithm on YAML front matter
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md lines 3, 25, 131, 133, 221 as 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. These lines are YAML description fields, feature lists, parameter tables, and section headers. No cryptographic algorithms are present anywhere in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines are plain markdown text with no cryptographic functions, imports, or operations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/shell backtick execution on markdown code fences
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 40 locations in SKILL.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. All flagged locations are standard markdown fenced code blocks (triple backticks) containing Python examples and bash documentation commands. These are documentation examples, not executable shell backtick commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown triple-backtick code blocks are standard documentation syntax. No actual shell command injection or Ruby backtick execution present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Path traversal in security checklist
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md lines 246 and 249 as 'path traversal sequence'. These lines are part of a security checklist that discusses preventing path traversal attacks. The '../' string appears in a security control item, not in actual executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is explicitly documenting security controls against path traversal, which is the opposite of a malicious pattern.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL in source attribution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md line 7 for hardcoded URL. This is a legitimate GitHub source attribution link for the open-source skill repository. It is documented metadata, not an exfiltration or C2 endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a standard GitHub repository link clearly labeled as 'Source'. No suspicious endpoint or encoded payload.

low_findings.title: Code Quality: Non-matching imports in __init__.py
low_findings.description: scripts/__init__.py imports WesternBlotQuantifier, AnalysisResult, and BandRegion from main.py, but main.py only defines the class WBQuantifier. These imported classes do not exist, which will cause ImportError at runtime. This is a code quality bug, not a security vulnerability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct comparison of import statements against class definitions in main.py confirms the mismatch.

content.user_title: Quantify Western Blot Gel Bands Automatically
content.value_statement: Manual quantification of Western Blot bands is slow, subjective, and prone to human error in biomedical research. This skill automates band detection, performs densitometric analysis, and calculates normalized protein expression values relative to loading controls like GAPDH.
actual_capabilities: Detect protein band positions and intensities in Western Blot gel images
Calculate raw and background-corrected optical density values for each band
Normalize target protein expression relative to loading control proteins such as GAPDH
Export quantification results as structured CSV files for downstream analysis
Support batch processing of multiple gel images with consistent parameters
Run in demo mode to validate output format before processing real experimental data
limitations: Requires high-resolution grayscale gel images for accurate band detection
Lane auto-detection may be inaccurate for unevenly spaced or distorted gel lanes
Cannot distinguish between specific and non-specific binding without user guidance on band identity
Background correction is limited to rolling ball, median, and none methods

use_cases.title: Analyze Protein Expression in Treatment vs Control Groups
use_cases.description: A researcher compares p53 protein levels between treated and untreated cell samples. The skill detects bands on the Western Blot image, normalizes to GAPDH loading control, and outputs relative expression values for statistical comparison.
use_cases.target_user: Biomedical researcher analyzing protein expression changes

use_cases.title: Batch Process Multiple Western Blot Images
use_cases.description: A lab technician processes a set of 20 gel images from a large-scale experiment. The skill applies consistent detection thresholds and normalization parameters across all images, producing standardized CSV output for each gel.
use_cases.target_user: Lab technician handling high-throughput Western Blot data

use_cases.title: Validate Antibody Specificity in Replicate Experiments
use_cases.description: A graduate student runs replicate blots to confirm antibody specificity. The skill quantifies bands across replicate images and provides normalized intensity values to assess reproducibility of the antibody signal.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Single Image Quantification
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Western Blot image using the western-blot-quantifier skill. The image path is [image_file.png]. Use GAPDH as the loading control and detect bands for p53 protein. Output the normalized quantification results.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user analyzing a single gel image with one target protein

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Protein Panel Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the western-blot-quantifier to analyze [gel_image.png]. Set reference_bands to 'GAPDH' and target_bands to ['p53', 'Bcl-2', 'p21']. Use 8 lanes with auto-detection. Save results to 'output/panel_results.csv' and include normalized values for all targets.
prompt_templates.scenario: User analyzing multiple target proteins on one gel

prompt_templates.title: Batch Processing with Custom Parameters
prompt_templates.prompt: Run western-blot-quantifier on all PNG files in the 'experiment_data' folder. Use beta-actin as the loading control, set detection threshold to 0.15, use median background correction, and configure 6 lanes per gel. Save each result to the 'output' folder with filenames matching the source image names.
prompt_templates.scenario: User processing multiple gels with adjusted detection parameters

prompt_templates.title: Custom Lane Positions for Distorted Gels
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the western-blot-quantifier to [crooked_gel.png]. The gel lanes are unevenly spaced so auto-detection may fail. Manually specify lane positions at [0.15, 0.35, 0.55, 0.75]. Use Tubulin as loading control, target_bands ['AKT'], rolling ball background correction, and save figures showing detected band regions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user troubleshooting non-ideal gel images with manual lane specification

output_examples.input: Western Blot image showing 4 lanes with GAPDH (loading control) and p53 bands
Batch processing request for 3 gel images with beta-actin loading control
output: experiment_1.png: 6 lanes, 2 targets (AKT, mTOR) - normalized values computed
experiment_2.png: 6 lanes, 2 targets (AKT, mTOR) - normalized values computed
experiment_3.png: 6 lanes, 2 targets (AKT, mTOR) - normalized values computed
All results saved to output/ directory as CSV files.
Warning: experiment_2.png lane 4 band intensity below threshold - verify manually.

best_practices: Always verify detected band positions visually before accepting quantification results to confirm auto-detection worked correctly
Use consistent loading controls across all samples in an experiment to ensure accurate relative expression comparisons
Document detection thresholds and background correction methods used so results remain reproducible
anti_patterns: Do not use low-resolution or heavily compressed JPEG images as band detection accuracy degrades significantly below 300 DPI
Do not compare normalized values across different experiments with different loading controls or detection thresholds
Do not skip manual lane position verification when gels show uneven sample loading or distorted band shapes

faq.question: What input image formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill works best with high-resolution grayscale or black-and-white PNG and TIFF images. JPEG compression artifacts can reduce band detection accuracy. Minimum recommended resolution is 300 DPI.

faq.question: How are loading controls used in normalization?
faq.answer: The skill divides the raw intensity of each target protein band by the intensity of the loading control band in the same lane. This corrects for variations in sample loading across lanes.

faq.question: What background correction methods are available?
faq.answer: Three methods are supported: rolling_ball (subtracts a smoothed background surface), median (applies median filter subtraction), and none (uses raw values). Rolling ball is the default.

faq.question: Can I analyze images with multiple target proteins per lane?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill accepts a list of target protein names. It detects all bands in each lane and matches them to the named targets based on expected molecular weight positions.

faq.question: What happens if lane auto-detection fails?
faq.answer: You can manually specify lane positions as relative values between 0 and 1 (as fractions of image width). This bypasses auto-detection and uses your provided positions.

faq.question: Does this skill make external network requests?
faq.answer: No. The skill runs entirely locally with no external API calls, network requests, or data transmission. All computation is performed on the input image file you provide.
